title: Growing tomatoes from seed
subtitle: The full sowing-to-plant-out arc on the UK calendar
excerpt: Tomato seed indoors in late February; pricked-out seedlings on a south-facing windowsill through March and April; hardened off in May; in the ground or pot by the end of the month. The four-month route to a UK tomato crop.
sourceNotes: RHS tomato page (https://www.rhs.org.uk/vegetables/tomatoes/grow-your-own) for the UK sowing window and hardening-off rhythm. Mrs Earle, Pot-Pourri from a Surrey Garden (1897), Project Gutenberg, for the original under-glass tomato culture. USDA agricultural extension fact sheets on indoor-sowing and damping-off prevention, public domain. Cross-checked with Joy Larkcom's Grow Your Own Vegetables (out of copyright passages on transplanting cues; modern editions paraphrased).

content.text: Tomatoes (Solanum lycopersicum) are the canonical frost-tender kitchen-garden plant. In a UK climate, they need a four-month head-start indoors before they go outside, and they need that head-start to be steady — uneven heat, uneven light, or a single cold night beyond fleece-protection range can set a tomato seedling back a fortnight. This guide takes the plant from dry seed in late February to a hardened-off plant in the ground or a pot at the end of May.

The route described here is the standard temperate-UK indoor-sown protocol. It applies cleanly across the EU and to similar climates in the Pacific Northwest of the US. Southern-Hemisphere readers should shift by six months; cold-winter US zones (USDA-3 and colder) start indoors later than late February so the plants aren't pot-bound by the time the last frost passes.
When to sow

Late February through late March is the UK indoor-sowing window. The cue is daylight: by late February most parts of the UK have crossed the 10-hour daylight threshold, which is enough for tomato seedlings to grow on a south-facing windowsill without going leggy. Earlier than that and the plants stretch for light; later than mid-April and the season runs out before the last fruit ripens.

Plant out around the last expected frost date for your area — late May in southern UK, first or second week of June in northern UK and Scotland. RHS frost-date maps are the cleanest reference; the BBC Weather seven-day frost forecast is the practical check for the planting weekend.
Where to sow

Module trays or small individual pots (7-9 cm) with a peat-free seed compost. The compost should be sieved if it's lumpy — tomato seed is small enough that a clod can hold a seedling underground. Fill the modules to within 1 cm of the rim and firm gently with a flat hand.

Soil temperature is the germination gate: tomato seed germinates reliably at 20-25°C. A heated propagator (the basic plug-in kind from a hardware shop) holds the temperature steady; a sunny windowsill in late February will be cooler than that overnight and germination will be slow and uneven without one. The propagator can be turned off once the seedlings are up.
Depth and spacing
Sow at 5 mm depth — one seed per module is the disciplined approach; two seeds per module and
pricking out

the weaker is the insurance approach. Cover with a fine sieve of seed compost or vermiculite, then a clear plastic dome or a sheet of clingfilm to hold humidity. Remove the cover as soon as the first seedlings show, which is usually 6-10 days.

Spacing at this stage doesn't matter much — modules are kept at touching distance until pricking out. Final planting-out spacing is 60 cm between plants in the ground; for pots, one plant per 30-litre container minimum.
Germination cues
The seedling emerges with two oval cotyledons. These look like a pair of small smooth leaves, but they're seed-storage tissue, not
true leaves
. The true leaves come 7-10 days later: the divided, serrated, slightly hairy adult shape.

Seed that hasn't shown above the compost after 14 days at a steady 20°C has failed. The common cause is old seed — tomato seed loses viability after about four years in a cool dry envelope. Fresh seed from a reputable supplier germinates at 90% or better; envelope-bottom seed from five years ago can drop to 30% with no visible difference in the dry seed.
Aftercare to transplant

Once the cotyledons are up, the seedlings need full light. Rotate the tray a quarter-turn every day to stop the plants leaning toward the window; on dull March days move them to the brightest pane in the house regardless of which room. Water from below where possible — sit the modules in a shallow tray of water for five minutes, then drain. Watering from above on cool damp compost is the route to damping off.

When the first true leaves are fully open (around day 21), prick out into individual 9 cm pots. Hold the seedling by a cotyledon, not the stem; lift the root ball with a pencil or a plant label. Plant deep — tomato stems root along their length, so the cotyledons can sit at compost level and the buried stem will produce a stronger root system. Water in and return to the windowsill.
Pot on again at around day 50 into a 13 cm pot or a 2-litre pot if you have them. The plants are 20-30 cm tall by mid-May and starting to crowd the windowsill — this is the cue to start
hardening off

Hardening off runs for 7-14 days. Day 1: outside for two hours in dappled shade, brought back in. Each day, add an hour outside and a little more direct sun. Day 7: outside all day, brought in at sunset. Day 14: outside day and night, with fleece thrown over if the forecast carries a late frost. After that, plant out — into the ground, into a 30-litre pot, or into a grow-bag with 2 plants.
What can go wrong

attrs.intro: The five ways tomato-from-seed sowing breaks down, and what to do.

items.fix: Turn the propagator off as soon as seedlings show. Move to the brightest available pane; rotate the tray daily. Re-pot deeper at the first-true-leaf stage so the buried stem can root and re-establish the plant.
items.cause: Insufficient light or too much heat after germination.

items.fix: Water from below not above. Don't overwater; let the top of the compost dry between drinks. Ensure airflow — open the propagator vents once seedlings are up. There's no save for a plant that's already collapsed; pull it and reseed if the season allows.
items.cause: Wet compost, cool temperatures, fungal infection at the soil line.
items.symptom: Damping off — seedlings collapse at the base, flop and rot within a day

items.fix: Heat the propagator to a steady 20-25°C. Use fresh seed from a reputable supplier (date on the packet). Sow at 5 mm — deeper, the seedling exhausts itself before reaching light.
items.cause: Cold compost, old seed, or buried too deep.

items.fix: Pot on into a fresh richer compost at the first-true-leaf stage. Check drainage — modules sitting in water for hours rot the roots and starve the leaves.
items.cause: Compost low in nutrients (peat-free seed composts are intentionally low-fertility), or overwatered.

items.fix: Pot on a stage earlier. By mid-April most varieties have outgrown a 9 cm pot and need to move to 13 cm or 2 litres.
items.cause: Pot-bound — the roots have circled the pot and run out of room.
items.symptom: Plants stop growing in early April

attrs.heading: If something went sideways

content.text: Variety pointers

Start with an established UK-adapted variety. Gardener's Delight is the reliable cherry-on-vine that's been the British starter tomato for fifty years; Moneymaker is the standard salad tomato; San Marzano gives the paste tomato yield where the season is long enough. Avoid named modern proprietary varieties on a first attempt — the seed packets are double the price and the UK summers don't always supply the heat the varieties expect.

A variety-selection guide for tomatoes follows this one; see that guide for the full named-variety list. The seed is the cheap part of the process — the four months of windowsill time, compost, and pot-on stages are where the investment sits, and a poorly-chosen variety wastes all of it.
